source_paper_title: On the Convex Formulations of Robust Markov Decision Processes
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Grand_Cl_ment_2025, title={On the Convex Formulations of Robust Markov Decision Processes}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0284}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0284}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Grand-Clément, Julien and Petrik, Marek}, year={2025}, month=aug, pages={1681–1706} }
context:
Section 1 (Introduction), page 2; reiterated in Section 7 (Conclusion), page 22.

Surprisingly, there is no known convex optimization formulation for RMDPs, analogous to the linear programming formulation of MDPs.

We assert that this is an important problem in the field of RMDPs too.

First, it may be possible to obtain exact formulations for RMDPs, not based on regularization, for specific uncertainty sets such as \(\ell_p\)-balls or \(\phi\)-divergence balls around nominal transitions.
title: Derive an exact tractable convex program for robust Markov decision processes
background_latex:
A discounted robust Markov decision process (RMDP) is specified by finite sets of states \(S\) and actions \(A\), nonnegative rewards \(r_{sa}\in\mathbb{R}_+\) for \((s,a)\in S\times A\), a discount factor \(\gamma\in(0,1)\), an initial distribution \(\alpha\in\Delta(S)\), and an uncertainty set \(\mathcal{U}\subseteq (\Delta(S))^{S\times A}\) of plausible transition kernels \(P=(P_{sas'})\). A stationary policy is \(\pi\in\Pi:=(\Delta(A))^S\). For a fixed transition kernel \(P\), the discounted return of \(\pi\) is
\[
R(\pi,P)=\mathbb{E}_{\pi,P}\Big[\sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t\, r_{S_tA_t}\ \Big|\ S_0\sim\alpha\Big].
\]
The RMDP objective is the max–min problem
\[
\max_{\pi\in\Pi}\ \min_{P\in\mathcal{U}}\ R(\pi,P).
\]
A common tractable structure is rectangularity of \(\mathcal{U}\). Under sa-rectangularity one has \(\mathcal{U}=\prod_{(s,a)\in S\times A} \mathcal{U}_{sa}\) with \(\mathcal{U}_{sa}\subseteq\Delta(S)\) convex and compact, and the robust Bellman operator \(T:\mathbb{R}^S\to\mathbb{R}^S\) is
\[
T(v)_s = \max_{a\in A}\ \min_{p\in\mathcal{U}_{sa}}\Big\{ r_{sa}+\gamma\, p^\top v\Big\},\qquad s\in S.
\]
The fixed point \(v^\star\) of \(T\) yields an optimal robust value function and an optimal stationary policy. Unlike the nominal MDP case (where a linear program exists), the maps \(v\mapsto T(v)_s\) need not be convex or concave, so the feasible sets \(\{v\in\mathbb{R}^S: v\ge T(v)\}\) or \(\{v\in\mathbb{R}^S: v\le T(v)\}\) are not known to admit a straightforward convex description in general.
problem_definition_latex:
Find conditions and a polynomial-size convex optimization formulation (e.g., conic, geometric, or other convex program) whose optimal solutions encode an optimal robust stationary policy and the optimal robust value function for the discounted RMDP
\[
\max_{\pi\in\Pi}\ \min_{P\in\mathcal{U}}\ R(\pi,P),
\]
under classical rectangular uncertainty (in particular sa-rectangular or s-rectangular \(\mathcal{U}\)), without introducing an approximation scheme based on regularization (i.e., an exact convex reformulation of the original RMDP rather than a sequence of convex programs converging to it).
latex:
A discounted robust Markov decision process (RMDP) is specified by finite sets of states \(S\) and actions \(A\), nonnegative rewards \(r_{sa}\in\mathbb{R}_+\) for \((s,a)\in S\times A\), a discount factor \(\gamma\in(0,1)\), an initial distribution \(\alpha\in\Delta(S)\), and an uncertainty set \(\mathcal{U}\subseteq (\Delta(S))^{S\times A}\) of plausible transition kernels \(P=(P_{sas'})\). A stationary policy is \(\pi\in\Pi:=(\Delta(A))^S\). For a fixed transition kernel \(P\), the discounted return of \(\pi\) is
\[
R(\pi,P)=\mathbb{E}_{\pi,P}\Big[\sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t\, r_{S_tA_t}\ \Big|\ S_0\sim\alpha\Big].
\]
The RMDP objective is the max–min problem
\[
\max_{\pi\in\Pi}\ \min_{P\in\mathcal{U}}\ R(\pi,P).
\]
A common tractable structure is rectangularity of \(\mathcal{U}\). Under sa-rectangularity one has \(\mathcal{U}=\prod_{(s,a)\in S\times A} \mathcal{U}_{sa}\) with \(\mathcal{U}_{sa}\subseteq\Delta(S)\) convex and compact, and the robust Bellman operator \(T:\mathbb{R}^S\to\mathbb{R}^S\) is
\[
T(v)_s = \max_{a\in A}\ \min_{p\in\mathcal{U}_{sa}}\Big\{ r_{sa}+\gamma\, p^\top v\Big\},\qquad s\in S.
\]
The fixed point \(v^\star\) of \(T\) yields an optimal robust value function and an optimal stationary policy. Unlike the nominal MDP case (where a linear program exists), the maps \(v\mapsto T(v)_s\) need not be convex or concave, so the feasible sets \(\{v\in\mathbb{R}^S: v\ge T(v)\}\) or \(\{v\in\mathbb{R}^S: v\le T(v)\}\) are not known to admit a straightforward convex description in general.

Find conditions and a polynomial-size convex optimization formulation (e.g., conic, geometric, or other convex program) whose optimal solutions encode an optimal robust stationary policy and the optimal robust value function for the discounted RMDP
\[
\max_{\pi\in\Pi}\ \min_{P\in\mathcal{U}}\ R(\pi,P),
\]
under classical rectangular uncertainty (in particular sa-rectangular or s-rectangular \(\mathcal{U}\)), without introducing an approximation scheme based on regularization (i.e., an exact convex reformulation of the original RMDP rather than a sequence of convex programs converging to it).
location_in_paper: Section 1 (Introduction), page 2; reiterated in Section 7 (Conclusion), page 22.
open_problem_quote:
Surprisingly, there is no known convex optimization formulation for RMDPs, analogous to the linear programming formulation of MDPs.

We assert that this is an important problem in the field of RMDPs too.

First, it may be possible to obtain exact formulations for RMDPs, not based on regularization, for specific uncertainty sets such as \(\ell_p\)-balls or \(\phi\)-divergence balls around nominal transitions.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for existence/construction of an exact polynomial-size convex reformulation in broad generality (or broad classes of uncertainty sets), which is not naturally falsified by finite counterexamples; failure may depend on complexity-theoretic obstructions rather than a small explicit instance.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: For specific uncertainty sets (e.g., \(\ell_p\)-balls or divergence balls), one can represent candidate reformulations or variable changes and test them computationally by comparing their optimal value against robust value iteration on randomly generated small RMDPs, giving an automatic fitness signal. However, the space of possible exact convexifications is very open-ended and success would likely be limited to discovering promising transformations or formulations for special cases rather than resolving the general problem.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Grand-Cl\'ement–Petrik, 2023) frames a central gap between the nominal discounted MDP case—where the Bellman inequalities \(v\ge T_P(v)\) yield a polynomial-size LP—and the discounted robust case under classical rectangular uncertainty, where the robust Bellman operator \(T(v)_s=\max_a\min_{p\in\mathcal U_{sa}}\{r_{sa}+\gamma p^\top v\}\) is generally neither convex nor concave in \(v\). The paper’s main contribution is therefore approximate: it adds entropic regularization in the maximization over actions and applies an exponential change of variables to obtain a polynomial-size convex (often conic) program whose solutions converge to the robust optimum as the regularization parameter tends to 0. The open problem asks whether one can remove this regularization/limit and obtain an exact polynomial-size convex formulation for the original max–min RMDP.

Among forward citations, no work is identified as giving such an exact convex reformulation in full generality. The closest structural progress is "Tractable robust Markov decision processes", which sharpens the landscape of when Bellman fixed-point dynamic programming is valid: for compact convex uncertainty sets, s-rectangularity and sa-rectangularity are essentially the only models that are tractable for all rewards/policies. This supports focusing on rectangular sets but does not by itself yield an explicit conic/LP description of \(\{v: v\ge T(v)\}\) or a single convex program encoding \(v^\star\) and an optimal robust policy.

Several papers provide partial tools rather than a full convexification. Data-driven distance-based ambiguity-set work (and related \(\kappa\)-rectangular modeling) emphasizes that for many practically used \(\mathcal U_{sa}\) (e.g., norm or divergence balls), the inner minimization \(\min_{p\in\mathcal U_{sa}} p^\top v\) is a convex optimization problem with tractable duals, suggesting that if one could decouple the outer \(\max_a\) and the dependence of \(p\) on \(v\) in a convex way, a conic epigraph might be possible in special cases. Conic-duality approaches in robust constrained MDPs show that even when the inner adversary problem is SOCP-representable, coupling it with policy choice typically introduces bilinearities, pinpointing a key obstruction to a global convex program. Algorithmic works (policy gradients; IWOCS) exploit rectangularity and saddle-point structure for computation but do not provide exact polynomial-size convex formulations. Overall, based on these forward citations, the exact convex-program reformulation for discounted rectangular RMDPs without regularization remains open; promising directions include identifying uncertainty-set classes where \(v\mapsto \min_{p\in\mathcal U_{sa}} p^\top v\) admits a conic epigraph that interacts benignly with the \(\max_a\) (e.g., via extended formulations), or proving impossibility/size lower bounds for general rectangular sets.

sources.title: Tractable robust Markov decision processes
sources.source_url: N/A (not provided in citation analysis block)
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves a characterization of when robust MDP policy evaluation admits a dynamic-programming fixed-point formulation via rectangular extensions. For compact convex uncertainty sets \(\mathcal P\subseteq (\Delta(S))^{S\times A}\):
- \(\mathcal P\) is s-tractable (i.e. for all stationary \(\pi\) and all rewards \(r_{sas'}\), \(\min_{P\in\mathcal P}\mu^\top v^{\pi,P}=\mu^\top u^\pi\) where \(u^\pi\) is the fixed point of \(T^\pi(v)_s=\min_{P\in\mathcal P}\sum_a\pi_{sa}P_{sa}^\top(r_{sa}+\gamma v)\)) iff \(\mathcal P\) is s-rectangular: \(\mathcal P=\prod_{s\in S}\mathcal P_s\) (Theorem 2).
- \(\mathcal P\) is sa-tractable (analogous statement with \(\tilde T^\pi(v)_s=\sum_a\pi_{sa}\min_{p\in\mathcal P_{sa}} p^\top(r_{sa}+\gamma v)\)) iff \(\mathcal P\) is sa-rectangular: \(\mathcal P=\prod_{(s,a)}\mathcal P_{sa}\) (Theorem 4).
Under next-state-independent rewards \(r_{sas'}\equiv r_{sa}\), they give a weaker simultaneous-solvability condition (Theorem 5) that holds for r-rectangular and other non-rectangular sets, implying fixed-point solvability and strong duality/stationary optimal policies for convex \(\mathcal P\) (Theorems 6–7).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an exact polynomial-size convex optimization reformulation of discounted robust MDPs under classical rectangular uncertainty (s- or sa-rectangular), avoiding approximation/regularization. This paper does not provide a new explicit conic/LP-size convex program encoding the optimal robust policy/value for general rectangular RMDPs. Instead, it characterizes when dynamic programming (fixed-point Bellman equations) exactly solves policy evaluation and optimization, proving that among compact convex uncertainty sets, only s-rectangular/sa-rectangular sets are tractable in full generality. It also notes a convex program to compute a fixed point of a monotone contraction (Proposition 2), yielding a convex formulation for policy evaluation \(u^\pi\) (and similarly for \(u^\star\)) as \(\max\{\sum_s u_s: u\le T^\pi(u)\}\), but it does not develop this into a full polynomial-size convex program for the original max–min RMDP with explicit constraints for general \(\mathcal U_{sa}\) beyond the DP oracle/minimization. Thus it provides structural/complexity insight and a potential route (via fixed-point-as-convex-program) rather than the requested explicit exact tractable convex reformulation.

sources.title: Dynamic Programming for Epistemic Uncertainty in Markov Decision Processes
sources.source_url: N/A (not provided in citation analysis block)
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper characterizes when state-based dynamic programming (Bellman fixed-point equations) holds for ambiguity-averse MDPs where the transition kernel is random and the objective is a law-invariant risk measure \(\rho\) applied to the random return. In particular (Theorem 4.4): for monotone, law-invariant, non-constant \(\rho\), dynamic programming (Conditions 1–2) forces \(\rho\) to be \(\operatorname{ess\,inf}\) or \(\operatorname{ess\,sup}\) for static kernels, and \(\operatorname{ess\,inf}\), \(\operatorname{ess\,sup}\), or \(\mathbb{E}\) for resampled kernels. Theorem 4.1 then implies existence of optimal stationary policies and validity of value/policy iteration whenever Conditions 1–2 hold.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an exact polynomial-size convex optimization reformulation of discounted robust MDPs under classical rectangular uncertainty (sa- or s-rectangular), without regularization/approximation. This paper does not provide such a convex program for robust MDPs; instead it studies a broader ambiguity-averse framework and proves an impossibility/rigidity result: among monotone law-invariant risk measures compatible with state-based dynamic programming, only worst-case (\(\operatorname{ess\,inf}\), i.e., robust), best-case (\(\operatorname{ess\,sup}\)), and (in resampled case) expectation survive. This supports the idea that robust MDPs are essentially the only law-invariant DP-tractable epistemic-uncertainty model, but it does not resolve the convex-formulation question for robust MDPs (e.g., whether \(\{v: v\ge T(v)\}\) admits a polynomial-size conic description under sa-rectangular sets). At most, it provides conceptual constraints and points to an (appendix) convex program for computing \(V_{\nu,\rho}\) under additional convexity assumptions on \(\rho\), which is not the same as an exact convex reformulation of the robust max–min problem.

sources.title: Robust markov decision processes with data-driven, distance-based ambiguity sets
sources.theorem_or_result: The dissertation establishes asymptotic and finite-sample guarantees for data-driven robust MDPs with distance-based ambiguity sets under (s,a)-rectangular and s-rectangular uncertainty. Representative results include: for (s,a)-rectangular discounted RMDPs with ambiguity balls of radius \(\varepsilon_N\to 0\) and a distance satisfying a topology-metrization condition (Assumption 1 / generalized Pinsker inequality), the robust optimal value \(\tilde J^N(\varepsilon_N)\to J^*\) almost surely (Theorems 1,4) and robust-optimal policies are eventually optimal for the true MDP (Theorems 2,5); for finite \(N\), if a concentration inequality holds (Assumption 3), then with probability \(\ge 1-\gamma\), the out-of-sample value of the robust-optimal policy is at least the robust value \(J_P(\hat\pi^N)\ge \tilde J^N(\varepsilon_{N,\beta(\gamma)})\) (Theorems 3,6). For a family of distance-based s-rectangular ambiguity sets \(\mathcal P(\rho,\varepsilon)\), under lower-semicontinuity/convexity of the distance, for any \(\rho<\infty\) there exists \(\varepsilon^*\in[\varepsilon/m^{1/\rho},\varepsilon]\) such that the s-rectangular robust optimal value matches an (s,a)-rectangular one: \(\tilde J_\infty(\varepsilon^*)=\tilde J_\rho(\varepsilon)\) (Theorem 8).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an exact tractable convex optimization formulation (polynomial-size) for discounted robust MDPs under classical rectangular uncertainty, avoiding regularization-based approximations. This dissertation does not provide a new exact convex program for the robust Bellman fixed point or the max–min objective. Instead it (i) analyzes statistical properties of data-driven distance-ball ambiguity sets and (ii) discusses computational solution via robust value iteration, where each Bellman backup requires solving an inner minimization \(\min_{p\in\mathcal U_{sa}}\{r_{sa}+\gamma p^\top v\}\), which is convex for many distances and sometimes reducible to LP/1D concave maximization. These are algorithmic/structural results, not a global convex reformulation of the entire RMDP. Theorem 8 relates s-rectangular and (s,a)-rectangular models via matching robust optimal values by adjusting radii, but again does not yield a single convex program encoding \(v^*\) and an optimal policy for general rectangular \(\mathcal U\). Thus it is partial/indirect progress: it provides related tools (convex inner problems, continuity/compactness arguments) and clarifies tractability for specific ambiguity sets, but does not solve the convex-formulation open problem.

sources.title: A Family of -Rectangular Robust MDPs: Relative Conservativeness, Asymptotic Analyses, and Finite-Sample Properties
sources.theorem_or_result: From the title/abstract only: the paper introduces a parametric family of \(\kappa\)-rectangular robust MDPs (interpolating between existing rectangularity notions) and provides results comparing their conservativeness plus asymptotic and finite-sample properties of the resulting robust policies/values. No exact polynomial-size convex reformulation of the discounted robust control problem is indicated in the available abstract.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for conditions yielding an exact tractable convex program (e.g., conic/GP) that encodes an optimal robust stationary policy/value for discounted RMDPs under (sa- or s-) rectangular uncertainty, without approximation/regularization.

Based on the title/abstract, this citing paper appears to modify/extend the uncertainty model (a family of \(\kappa\)-rectangular ambiguity sets) and study statistical/conservativeness properties. That is related to robust MDP modeling and may clarify which rectangularity structures are overly conservative or statistically well-behaved, but it does not (from the abstract) provide an exact convex optimization reformulation of the robust Bellman fixed-point inequalities or the max–min objective. If the paper derives new tractable formulations for \(\kappa\)-rectangular sets (e.g., via LP/conic duality), that could be relevant, but this cannot be confirmed without the full text.

sources.title: Transition Uncertainties in Constrained Markov Decision Models: A Robust Optimization Approach
sources.source_url: N/A (not provided in citation analysis block)
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies a discounted robust constrained MDP with uncertain transition probabilities modeled as \,\(p=\bar p+u\)\, where \,\(u\)\, lies in an s-rectangular uncertainty set \,\(U\)\, defined by polyhedral constraints and second-order-cone constraints (ellipsoidal-type). For a fixed stationary policy \,\(f\), it shows each inner worst-case expectation problem \(\max_{u\in U} (1-\alpha)\gamma^T(I-\alpha P_f(u))^{-1}c_f\) is equivalent to an SOCP via a change of variables (Lemma 1), and by SOCP strong duality derives an equivalent reformulation of the overall robust CMDP as an SOCP with bilinear constraints in the policy and dual variables (Theorem 1; similarly Theorem 2 under an alternative positivity assumption).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an exact polynomial-size convex program for discounted robust MDPs under classical rectangular uncertainty (sa- or s-rectangular), avoiding regularization/approximation. This paper does not provide a convex reformulation of the robust MDP objective \(\max_\pi\min_P R(\pi,P)\). Instead it treats a more complex robust CMDP and derives an exact reformulation that remains nonconvex because of bilinear terms coupling the stationary policy \(f\) with variables like \(\bar P_f\) and \(F_f(s)\) in constraints (12b–c,12f–g). The work is relevant in that it uses conic duality for s-rectangular uncertainty sets and produces SOCP representations of the inner minimization/maximization, but it stops short of an exact tractable convex program for the outer robust control problem; indeed it explicitly notes the final formulation is non-convex and solved with global solvers.

sources.title: Policy Gradient Algorithms for Robust MDP with Nonrectangular Uncertainty Sets
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper develops first-order algorithms (projected Langevin dynamics, Frank–Wolfe, and actor–critic) for robust discounted MDPs with general convex (possibly non-rectangular) uncertainty sets \(\mathcal P\). Key guarantees include:
- Projected Langevin dynamics (Alg. 3.1) returns \(\xi\) with \(\mathbb E[V^{P_\xi}_\pi(s_0)]\ge V^\star_\pi(s_0)-\varepsilon\) after \(M\) iterations with \(M\) exponential in parameter dimension \(q\) (Thm. 3.6).
- A Frank–Wolfe method (Alg. 3.2) terminates in \(O(1/\varepsilon^2)\) iterations and outputs \(\hat P\) with suboptimality bound \(V^\star_\pi(s_0)-V^{\hat P}_\pi(s_0)\le \delta_d(2\varepsilon+\delta_{\mathcal P})\), where \(\delta_{\mathcal P}=0\) for \(s\)-rectangular sets (Thm. 3.12, Cor. 3.13).
- An actor–critic scheme (Alg. 4.1) finds an \(\epsilon\)-optimal robust policy improvement solution in \(O(1/\epsilon^4)\) iterations given an \(\varepsilon\)-accurate robust evaluation oracle (Thm. 4.5).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an exact polynomial-size convex optimization reformulation of discounted robust MDPs under classical rectangular uncertainty (\(sa\)- or \(s\)-rectangular), without approximation/regularization. This paper does not provide a convex program reformulation of the robust Bellman fixed point or the max–min control problem. Instead it proposes gradient-based algorithms (including for non-rectangular sets) and proves convergence/suboptimality bounds. For rectangular sets, it notes global optimality of its Frank–Wolfe evaluation method (Cor. 3.13), but this is an iterative first-order method, not an exact tractable convex program encoding \(v^\star\) and an optimal stationary policy. Thus it offers algorithmic tools and complexity/comparison results rather than the requested convex formulation.

sources.title: Soft robust mdps and risk-sensitive mdps: Equivalence, policy gradient, and sample complexity
sources.source_url: N/A (not provided in citation analysis block)
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves an equivalence between a class of “soft robust MDPs” with penalty-regularized adversarial transitions and a new formulation of risk-sensitive MDPs based on convex risk measures. In particular, if the penalty function and risk measure are Fenchel conjugates so that
\[D(\hat P_{sa},P_{sa})=\sup_V\big(-\sigma(P_{sa},V)-\mathbb E_{s'\sim \hat P_{sa}}V(s')\big),\]
then the soft robust value functions and Q-functions coincide with the risk-sensitive ones, and an optimal robust policy can be taken stationary deterministic:
- \(V^\star=\tilde V^\star\), \(Q^\star=\tilde Q^\star\)
- \(\pi^\star(s)=\arg\max_a\{r(s,a)-\gamma\sigma(P_{sa},V^\star)\}\).
It also derives a policy-gradient theorem for these objectives and gives global convergence of exact policy gradient under tabular direct parameterization, plus an offline algorithm (RFZI) with sample-complexity bounds for the KL-penalized (entropy-risk) case.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for an exact, polynomial-size tractable convex optimization formulation for the classical discounted rectangular RMDP max–min objective (hard uncertainty set \(\mathcal U=\prod_{sa}\mathcal U_{sa}\)) without approximation/regularization.

This paper does not provide such a convex program. Its main technical development concerns a different objective: a “soft” robust MDP where the adversary is regularized/penalized via \(D\) (e.g., KL), which the open problem explicitly excludes as an approximation scheme. While the authors note that choosing \(D\) as an indicator of a constraint set recovers the standard robust MDP, the paper’s equivalence and algorithms are developed primarily for the penalized formulation and for risk-sensitive Bellman equations, not for deriving an exact convex feasible set/LP/SOCP/SDP for the hard rectangular robust Bellman inequalities.

Nevertheless, the Fenchel-duality viewpoint (risk measure \(\leftrightarrow\) penalty) is a potentially relevant tool: it rewrites inner minimizations over transition kernels as conjugates of convex functionals, which could inspire convex-analytic reformulations in special cases (e.g., KL/\(\phi\)-divergence balls) but does not resolve the general exact-convex-formulation question posed in the original paper.

sources.title: Revisiting the static model in robust reinforcement learning
sources.source_url: N/A (not provided in citation analysis block)
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proposes the IWOCS meta-algorithm for robust RL under transition uncertainty. Using (i) the equivalence between static and dynamic adversaries for stationary policies under sa-rectangular uncertainty (Iyengar, 2005, Lemma 3.3) and (ii) a saddle-point/no-duality-gap condition (Wiesemann et al., 2013), it motivates solving 
\[\max_{\pi}\min_{T\in\mathcal T} V_T^\pi(s_0)=\min_{T\in\mathcal T} V_T^\*(s_0)\]
by iteratively: (a) solving standard (non-robust) MDPs for a growing finite set \(\mathcal T_i\subset\mathcal T\), (b) defining \(Q_i(s,a)=\min_{T\in\mathcal T_i} Q_T^\*(s,a)\), and (c) selecting a new worst-case model \(T_{i+1}\in\arg\min_{T\in\mathcal T} V_T^{\pi_i}(s_0)\). It establishes monotonicity and bounds: 
- \(Q_i(s,a)\ge Q^*_{\tilde{\mathcal T}_i}(s,a)\ge Q^*_{\mathcal T}(s,a)\) where \(\tilde{\mathcal T}_i\) is the sa-rectangular hull of \(\mathcal T_i\).
- \(Q_{i+1}(s,a)\le Q_i(s,a)\) for all \(s,a\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an exact, polynomial-size convex optimization reformulation (e.g., conic/geometric) that encodes the optimal robust stationary policy/value for discounted RMDPs under rectangular uncertainty, without approximation/regularization. This paper does not provide any convex program for the robust Bellman inequalities or the max–min objective. Instead it develops an algorithmic heuristic (IWOCS) that reduces robust optimization to repeated solutions of nominal MDPs plus a worst-case model search (black-box CMA-ES in continuous domains, brute force in a toy discrete example). The method is not an exact convex reformulation; it offers no guarantee of reaching the true robust optimum in general (the authors explicitly note convergence is not necessarily to \(Q^*_{\mathcal T}\)). Thus it does not solve the convex-formulation open problem, but is tangentially related as an alternative computational approach to robust RMDPs and highlights duality/saddle-point structure used in convex-analytic treatments.
